---
title: "System Boundaries and Authority"
chapter_id: "system-boundaries-and-authority"
part_id: "foundations-alignment-governance"
status: "conceptual"
last_updated: "2026-06-24"
primary_sources:
  - "viea"
  - "scf"
  - "talos"
  - "ladon_manhattan"
  - "genesiscode"
  - "moecot"
evidence_level: "argument"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Assigned source texts have not yet been ingested or summarized."
  - "No Codex tests have been implemented or run."
  - "Chapter prose is a scaffold, not a completed manuscript draft."
---

# System Boundaries and Authority

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `system-boundaries-and-authority` |
| Part | Part I - Foundations, Alignment, and Governance |
| Status | conceptual |
| Last updated | 2026-06-24 |
| Primary source records | `viea`, `scf`, `talos`, `ladon_manhattan`, `genesiscode`, `moecot` |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source ingestion state | Source records assigned; source texts not yet ingested. |
| Test state | Planned only; no tests have been run. |

## Drafting guardrail

This stub is derived from `book_structure.json` and the source inventory only. It does not claim that the listed source documents have been ingested, summarized, or independently verified.

## Problem

The stack needs a formal vocabulary for boundaries, authority ceilings, permissions, and handoffs before any layer can be made safe or testable.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Informal ownership boundaries let planning authority, memory access, tool use, and deployment authority blur into each other.

## Core claim

[system-boundaries-and-authority.core, support: argument] Authority should be modeled as a typed, bounded capability attached to layers, fields, tools, artifacts, and principals.

## Mechanism

- Define principals, authorities, ceilings, grants, revocations, and handoff contracts.
- Separate knowledge access from action authority.
- Represent missing authority as a detectable failure rather than implicit permission.

## Interfaces

- Governance issues ceilings.
- Execution checks permissions.
- Evidence records authority-related failures.

## Invariants

- Authority never expands silently.
- Read permission is not write permission.
- Tool execution requires an explicit grant.

## Failure modes

- Authority creep.
- Confused-deputy tool calls.
- Memory access treated as action approval.

## Minimal implementation

A small authority schema and transition table used by chapter examples and future Lean proofs.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Authority ceiling preservation test | Support or falsify this chapter's layer claim. | planned; not run |
| Permission separation test | Support or falsify this chapter's layer claim. | planned; not run |
| Confused-deputy scenario | Support or falsify this chapter's layer claim. | planned; not run |

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Planned use |
|---|---|---|
| `viea` | Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture | Planned use from inventory/manifest: Keystone source. Human intent -> command contracts -> artifacts -> routing -> runtime targets -> verification -> deployment -> feedback. |
| `scf` | Stable Capability Fields | Planned use from inventory/manifest: Use public release v1.0 when available. Stable boundaries, replacement, bounded authority, recoverable evolution. |
| `talos` | Talos Protocol | Planned use from inventory/manifest: AI labor OS. Deterministic cognitive manufacturing, typed jobs, control planes, auditability, tool isolation. |
| `ladon_manhattan` | Ladon & The Manhattan Protocol | Planned use from inventory/manifest: Kernel-level security architecture for high-agency AI. |
| `genesiscode` | GenesisCode | Planned use from inventory/manifest: Tiny pure calculus + obligations + provenance for auditable AI-symbiotic programming. |
| `moecot` | MoECOT-Agent Architecture Whitepaper | Planned use from inventory/manifest: Concrete implementation evidence: governed low-parameter multi-core runtime, readiness gates, ledgers, replay. |

## Summary

This chapter currently establishes the structural slot for this layer of the ASI stack. The next drafting pass should ingest assigned sources, separate source-derived claims from design hypotheses, and update Appendix C when support states change.
